text:
A gang of goblins on rusty scooters circles you, engines buzzing like angry wasps. Their leader flips up her goggles: 'Tribute. Or race. Them's the roads.'

A plaque may eventually misdescribe "Road Scooter Gang". For now, the useful truth is that the authorities call this smuggling because rescue would imply a duty.

success.text: You win on a borrowed scooter with a wobbling wheel. The gang is furious and deeply impressed. They pay tribute to YOU, as is the law of the roads.
You race on your own machine, which changes everything. You take the leader on the last bend and the gang goes silent, then feral with joy. Tribute flows to YOU, plus a respectful oil change.

fail.text: You crash into the one bush on an otherwise empty plain. The gang laughs so hard two of them also crash. Tribute is waived for entertainment value, but the bush wants damages.
Rain has turned the course to soup. You go down in the mud at the first bend, along with half the gang, out of solidarity with physics. Tribute is waived. Laundry is not.

outcomes.text: The leader stamps your hand: road tax, paid. 'Valid till the next rain,' she says. It begins raining immediately.
The leader squints, then yanks her goggles off entirely. 'It's YOU.' Tribute is refused, an autograph is demanded, and you are escorted a mile up the road with full honk honors.

success.text: There's a form. Of course there's a form. You're now an associate member with scooter privileges and a cut of today's tribute.
They've seen your poster. The leader pins it to the clubhouse board on the spot, which is apparently the gang equivalent of a diploma. Membership is immediate and enthusiastic.

fail.text: 'We have standards,' says the goblin holding a scooter together with rope. The rejection stings more than it should.

text:
A merchant sits beside a wagon with a shattered wheel, surrounded by crates labeled 'FRAGILE,' 'VERY FRAGILE,' and 'DO NOT ASK.'

The argument around "Road Broken Wagon" ends wherever someone finally admits that the practical choice is cruelty; the useful choice may be refusing that definition of practical.

success.text: The wheel goes on, the merchant beams, and you're paid from the DO NOT ASK crate. It's money. You don't ask.
Fixed! The merchant pays you in 'credits' - shiny rectangles from beyond the sky. 'Big somewhere,' they insist.

The road is mud and the jack keeps sinking, so you do it the ugly way: shoulder under the axle in the rain while the merchant screams encouragement. Double pay from DO NOT ASK, no questions either direction.

fail.text: The wagon rolls off the jack and onto your foot. The merchant apologizes on behalf of gravity.
The wagon slides off the jack, through the mud, and onto your foot with slow, greasy inevitability. The merchant apologizes on behalf of gravity and weather both.

outcomes.text: 'The first two are pottery.' You wait. The merchant stares through you. 'The pottery is also not to be asked about.' You move on, enriched only by mystery.

choices.text: Loot a crate while they're distracted

success.text: You palm something small from DO NOT ASK. It's warm. It hums. It's worth money to somebody, ideally somebody far away.

fail.text: The crate SHRIEKS. Whatever's inside does not appreciate being burgled. The merchant charges you an 'awakening fee.'
The crate SHRIEKS. Your companion, bless them, fakes a coughing fit so committed that the merchant only charges half the awakening fee, out of concern.

choices.text: Hand over your repair kit and supervise

outcomes.text: Proper tools change the whole afternoon. The wheel goes on true, the merchant keeps the kit 'for emergencies,' and pays what a wheelwright would charge plus a mystery bonus from the middle crate.

text:
A peddler's cart lurches to a stop beside you. The whole thing unfolds like a wooden flower into a shopfront. Impressive. Structurally alarming, but impressive.

Behind the business of "Road Peddler" sits an older truth: the market is invisible except when it needs guards, roads, courts, or lunch.

outcomes.text: 'Road prices,' the peddler warns, meaning higher.
The demonstration re-folds the cart with the peddler inside. You help extract them. A discount is mentioned, emotionally, but a free sample is provided.
'Sky-metal hinges,' they whisper. 'Fell off a star-wagon.' Everything is sky-debris to salespeople now.

The hinges are frozen. The cart half-unfolds, groans, and sticks, wedged open like a wooden crab. You spend an hour thawing sky-metal with the peddler, who pays in something hot and one apologetic discount item.
The cart refolds and trundles off, playing a tinny jingle that will live in your head for three days.

The peddler packs up fast when the wind carries drums. 'Roads are only roads until they're routes,' they mutter, folding the shopfront away, and trundle off in the exact opposite direction of the smoke.

text:
A wizard stands at the roadside, thumb out, beard tucked into his belt for travel. His sign reads 'ANYWHERE BUT HERE. WILL ENCHANT FOR RIDES.'

The paperwork around "Road Hitchhiking Wizard" avoids its central fact: nobody expects help, making help both naive and tactically surprising.

outcomes.text: He rides side-saddle with tremendous dignity and enchants your scooter in payment. It now refuses to tip over and hums show tunes.
Two miles of wizard small talk, which is regular small talk but about portents. He teaches you a cantrip for keeping boots dry. Life-changing, honestly.
It is raining, which is the single best sales environment for a boot-drying cantrip. He demonstrates on yours mid-puddle. You walk two miles warm-footed through a downpour, converted for life.
He talks the entire way about his ex-apprentice. THE ENTIRE WAY. At the crossroads he pays you for the therapy in coin, as is proper.
He looks back down the road. Far away, faintly, something honks. 'Geese,' he says. 'A grudge. Don't ask more.' You don't. The honking continues, patient.
He looks back down the road, where the smoke is. 'Recruiters,' he says. 'They want battle-wizards. I do boots.' He teaches you the ditch-spotting trick he uses whenever wagons with banners come by.

choices.text: Ask him to do something about your sword

outcomes.text: He tuts at the rust, mutters at the blade, and hands back something a smith would charge real money for. 'Edge-keeping charm. Do not thank me, it embarrasses the geese.'
The spell takes the rust off, along with a certain amount of sword. What remains is technically a very committed dagger. He insists this is an upgrade in spirit.

text:
A gleaming pillar of sky-metal blocks the road. It projects glowing text: 'TOLL: 5 CREDITS. CASH NOT ACCEPTED. HAVE A PRODUCTIVE CYCLE.' It has clearly been here since before anyone knew what credits were.

Behind the business of "Road Automated Toll" sits an older truth: a post-scarcity tool has been fitted with a coin slot for cultural compatibility.

outcomes.text: You wave the blinking chip. The pillar chimes, thanks you for your 'loyalty,' and awards points you can never spend.

success.text: You convince the pillar you're a maintenance worker. It apologizes, opens, and pays YOU a service fee from its dusty internal purse.

fail.text: The pillar declares an 'unauthorized access event' and tases you politely. It then offers a customer satisfaction survey.

You attempt the hack in the rain, wet-fingered, on a machine that runs on lightning. The pillar does not even need to aim. The survey afterward includes a question about 'moisture-related user error.'

choices.text: Walk around it through the brush

outcomes.text: The pillar watches you the whole way, projecting the word 'NOTED.' The brush is full of thorns and the moral high ground.

Halfway through the brush, the pillar's light sweeps over you and stops. 'IDENTITY MATCHED: BOUNTY DATABASE.' It projects your poster at parade size for every traveler on the road. The thorns are now the second-worst part.

'IDENTITY MATCHED: PERSON OF NOTE.' The pillar plays a fanfare from a civilization that no longer exists and opens with what can only be described as reverence. It asks you to rate your passage. You give it five.

text:
Dusk. A good sheltered spot presents itself: flat stone, dry wood, the kind of quiet that makes camp sound like wisdom.

Ask who pays for "Wild Campfire" and the answer begins here: a small mercy cannot repair the system, but it can keep one person alive to organize tomorrow.

outcomes.text: Fire, food, and the deep sleep of someone who packed the right equipment. Morning finds you new.
Fire in falling snow: the kit earns its weight twice over. You sleep in a dome of orange quiet while the world is erased around you, and wake to a clean page.
The kit, plus someone to split the watch. You get the deep half of the night and wake to breakfast already going. Civilization is a fire with two people at it.

success.text: Cold ground, warm cloak, decent stars. You wake stiff but restored.

fail.text: It rains. The shelter that looked wise redirects the water directly onto you, with craftsmanship.
It was already raining when you lay down, which even you recognize as a decision. The shelter funnels the entire county's weather onto your bedroll by the small hours.
Winter ground takes heat the way a debt collector takes coin: entirely, and with interest. You spend the night doing arithmetic about dawn.

outcomes.text: You make good time in the dark and feel like a legend. An exhausted legend.
Night riding: cold, fast, and gloriously stupid. You cover double distance with your eyes streaming and arrive before your reputation does.
Night travel introduces you to a ditch. Personally.

text:
A funeral procession fills the road - for {gone}, dead these many years, whose remains are only now being moved to a fancier grave. The mourners are professionals; the grief is rented; the traffic is real.

The file titled "Road Funeral Procession" makes the arrangement plain: the emergency is obvious, but compassion has not completed the required procurement form.

choices.text: Join the procession for a while

outcomes.text: You walk a mile behind the casket of a stranger. The professional mourners teach you two harmonies and a surprising amount about {gone}. Attendance includes a wake-lunch.

Rain is a gift to the profession. Umbrellas bloom in black rows, and your unfeigned misery at being soaked reads as top-shelf grief. The head mourner offers you occasional work, and the wake-lunch is doubled.
You thread the gap between casket and clergy at a jog. Gasps. Someone faints professionally. You clear it, pursued only by reputation.
You take the gap at speed with the engine snarling. The procession will describe this event for years. So will the wanted-poster artist for the temple district, working from six eyewitness sketches.
You misjudge the pallbearers' pace. The collision is slow, ceremonial, and witnessed by everyone. {gone}'s remains are fine. Your standing is not.

choices.text: Ask why the upgrade after all these years

success.text: A mourner breaks character: the estate finally settled, and the lawyers took everything but the grave budget. You get the whole saga and a tip for listening to THEIR grief about it.

The head mourner recognizes you and breaks character entirely. The whole saga comes out, plus an offer: walk graveside as a Celebrity Mourner. Your presence upgrades the funeral's prestige tier, and the estate pays appearance rates.

fail.text: 'Ghoul,' hisses the head mourner, misunderstanding completely. You are moved along by six umbrellas.

text:
A crossroads signpost with one arm too many. Three point to real places. The fourth, freshly painted, reads 'DEFINITELY NO TREASURE THIS WAY (BORING) (DO NOT)'. The handwriting is familiar. Nervous.

Nothing about "Road Signpost Liar" is accidental once you notice that the official account is polished, confident, and contradicted by every person who was there.

outcomes.text: The path winds into deep green quiet. Someone worked hard to keep people away from here.

choices.text: Follow a real sign like a sensible person

outcomes.text: You take the boring road. It is boring. It is also entirely free of whatever that other path was about, and some days that's the whole victory.

The boring road is full of soldiers' wagons and requisitioned mules. Boring is over in this county. You give the columns a wide berth along the field edges, which costs you daylight and one boot's dignity.
Snow has buried every road equally, but the boring one has fresh cart ruts to walk in. Whoever went first did the hard part, which is the entire point of civilization.
You paint 'FREE SOUP AHEAD' pointing into a swamp. It's cruel. It's hilarious. Somewhere down the line, a berry-goblin nods in professional respect.
Your companion watches you paint 'FREE SOUP AHEAD' with the expression of someone updating their opinion of you in real time. They fix your kerning anyway. Everyone needs a hobby with witnesses.
